prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a long calm row of identical, content little data-point characters strolling peacefully along a flat path, all bored and unremarkable, while one single point near the end has leapt high into the air in a startled pose, instantly drawing the wide-eyed attention of a friendly inspector character peering through a magnifying glass; gentle wavy guide-lines suggest a time series flowing left to right. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. Suitable for a technical textbook. The humor is dry and immediately readable: surprise is the only currency a monitor trades in. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A long row of calm identical data-point characters walking a flat path while one near the end leaps high in surprise, drawing a magnifying-glass inspector's attention, dramatizing how an anomaly is simply the point that fails to belong.
caption: An anomaly earns attention not by being wrong but by being surprising, and surprise is the only currency a monitor trades in.

after_heading: The Three Types: Point, Contextual, and Collective Anomalies
prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a split scene showing the very same cheerful beach umbrella twice; on the sunny left half it stands perfectly normal over a relaxed sunbather on warm sand, while on the snowy right half the identical umbrella stands absurdly over a shivering figure in a swirling blizzard, looking wildly out of place though nothing about the umbrella itself changed. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. Suitable for a technical textbook. The humor is dry and immediately readable: identical value, different context, only one is an anomaly. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: The same beach umbrella shown twice, perfectly normal on sunny sand but absurdly out of place in a blizzard, illustrating a contextual anomaly where an unremarkable value becomes anomalous only once its temporal context is taken into account.
caption: A contextual anomaly is a perfectly ordinary value in the wrong season: the umbrella never changed, only the context that made it normal did.

prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a stern but well-meaning bouncer character stands at a single firm rope line drawn across a cloud of scattered point characters; the points safely below the rope mill about happily admitted, while the few points that drifted above the rope are being firmly waved aside as outliers, the bouncer pointing decisively upward; a couple of borderline points hover right at the rope looking nervous. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. Suitable for a technical textbook. The humor is dry and immediately readable: a threshold draws one hard line and everything past it is flagged. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A bouncer character at a single rope line admits the cluster of points below it while firmly waving aside the few points that drifted above, with borderline points hovering nervously at the rope, dramatizing how a statistical threshold draws one hard line and flags everything past it.
caption: A statistical threshold is a bouncer with one rope: everything past the line is flagged, which makes where you draw the line the whole game.

prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a walking character strolls confidently along a level floor that, at one clear point behind them, has quietly tilted and locked into a new steeper slope; the character is still walking as if everything is fine and only now beginning to glance back over one shoulder, dawning realization on their face that the ground itself changed a few steps ago and never changed back. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. Suitable for a technical textbook. The humor is dry and immediately readable: a change point is a persistent shift noticed too late. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A character walks along a floor that has tilted to a new permanent slope a few steps behind them, only now glancing back in dawning realization, illustrating how a change point is a lasting regime shift in the data-generating process rather than a brief one-off anomaly.
caption: A change point is the moment the floor tilts to a new slope and stays there; the hard part is that you usually notice a few steps too late.

prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a confident forecaster character stands pointing forward along a smooth predicted curve drawn as a calm dotted arc reaching ahead, beaming with certainty, while the actual signal arriving has just shot far above that arc, leaving a huge dramatic gap between where the forecaster pointed and where reality landed; an alert little flag-bearer character pops up exactly in that gap to mark the surprise. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. Suitable for a technical textbook. The humor is dry and immediately readable: the size of the gap between prediction and reality is the anomaly score. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A confident forecaster points along a smooth predicted arc while the actual signal shoots far above it, leaving a large gap that a small alert character flags, dramatizing how a forecasting residual scores an anomaly as the distance between prediction and reality.
caption: Score the surprise: the gap between what your forecaster confidently expected and what actually arrived is itself the anomaly score.

prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: a friendly machine character squeezes a familiar, smoothly repeating wavy shape down through a narrow funnel into a tiny compact bead and then rebuilds it almost perfectly on the far side, pleased with itself; but when an oddly jagged unfamiliar shape is fed into the same funnel, the rebuilt version on the far side comes out mangled and obviously wrong, the machine looking puzzled at the mismatch between input and output. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. Suitable for a technical textbook. The humor is dry and immediately readable: what the compressor cannot rebuild is the anomaly. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: A machine squeezes a familiar repeating wave through a narrow funnel and rebuilds it perfectly, but a jagged unfamiliar shape comes out mangled, illustrating reconstruction-based detection where whatever a compact representation cannot rebuild is flagged as anomalous.
caption: Reconstruction detection flags whatever the compressor cannot rebuild; the everyday shape passes through cleanly, the strange one comes back mangled, and that gap is the alarm.

after_heading: The Operational Reality: Alert Fatigue and the Triage Loop
prompt: A witty cartoon-like educational illustration with clean lines and a warm color palette: an exhausted slumped operator character is half-buried under a blizzard of identical little harmless alarm bells raining down from every direction, eyes glazed and ignoring them all out of sheer fatigue; unnoticed at the very edge of the pile, one single larger bell glowing a warm urgent red, the one real emergency, rings unheard. Style: friendly and approachable like a clever XKCD or Kurzgesagt panel, expressive faces and body language, one clear visual gag, minimal clutter, generous negative space. Suitable for a technical textbook. The humor is dry and immediately readable: a flood of false alarms buries the one alert that mattered. No text, letters, numbers, or labels anywhere in the image.
alt: An exhausted operator buried under a blizzard of identical harmless alarm bells ignores them all, missing the single glowing red bell at the edge that signals the real emergency, dramatizing how alert fatigue from false alarms causes the one true alert to be missed.
caption: The real adversary in production is not a missed anomaly but alert fatigue: bury an operator in false alarms and the one that mattered rings unheard.
